# Literature Review: dexamethasone airway smooth muscle transcriptomics

*8 papers retrieved from PubMed | Generated by Literature Review Agent*

---

## Overview

This structured review synthesizes findings from eight PubMed abstracts examining the transcriptomic effects of dexamethasone on airway smooth muscle (ASM) cells in the context of asthma and related airway diseases. The studies span from 1995 to 2025 and collectively employ a range of genomic and transcriptomic approaches—including RNA sequencing, microarray profiling, chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-Seq), and quantitative PCR—to characterize glucocorticoid-responsive gene networks in ASM. The overarching research landscape reflects an evolving understanding of how dexamethasone and other glucocorticoids modulate ASM gene expression, with implications for both therapeutic efficacy and steroid resistance in asthma.

## Key Findings

A central and replicated finding across multiple studies was the identification of Krüppel-like factor 15 (KLF15) as a prominent glucocorticoid receptor (GR) target in ASM. Masuno and colleagues demonstrated that dexamethasone treatment of human ASM (HASM) cells produced statistically distinguishable changes in nearly 7,500 genes after 24 hours, with KLF15 emerging as an early transcriptional target; loss of KLF15 in a murine ovalbumin model was associated with diminished airway hyperresponsiveness, and its overexpression reduced HASM cell proliferation. This finding was subsequently extended by Sasse and colleagues, who employed GR ChIP-Seq alongside transcriptome data from KLF15 overexpression to identify phospholipase C delta 1 (PLCD1) as both a KLF15-regulated gene and a repressor of ASM hypertrophy, while also establishing additional direct GR transcriptional targets in ASM, including IRS2, APPL2, RAMP1, and MFGE8. Together, these studies suggest that KLF15 occupies a central node in the glucocorticoid-regulated transcriptional network governing ASM structure and function.

Beyond KLF15 signaling, transcriptomic studies identified a range of novel glucocorticoid-responsive genes with relevance to inflammation. Himes and colleagues applied RNA-Seq to four primary human ASM cell lines treated with dexamethasone (1 µM for 18 hours) and identified 316 differentially expressed genes, including previously known targets such as DUSP1, PER1, and TSC22D3, as well as the less-investigated secreted protein CRISPLD2. Functional follow-up demonstrated that CRISPLD2, previously implicated in lung development and endotoxin regulation, was upregulated by both dexamethasone and the inflammatory cytokine IL-1β; small interfering RNA-mediated knockdown of CRISPLD2 amplified IL-1β-induced expression of IL-6 and IL-8, suggesting an anti-inflammatory modulatory role for this gene. Additionally, single nucleotide polymorphisms in CRISPLD2 were found to be moderately associated with inhaled corticosteroid resistance and bronchodilator response, positioning it as a candidate pharmacogenomics gene.

An important and potentially clinically consequential finding emerged from the study by Ouyang and colleagues, who used RNA sequencing of human ASM cells treated with IL-17A, dexamethasone, or their combination. Their analysis identified a subset of genes synergistically induced by IL-17A and dexamethasone, most notably CSF3, which encodes a neutrophil-promoting cytokine. In type-17 preclinical models of neutrophilic severe asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, dexamethasone treatment alone failed to alleviate neutrophilic inflammation and in some contexts exacerbated the disease phenotype through CSF3 upregulation; disruption of IL-17A/dexamethasone synergy via IL-17A inhibition or depletion of CSF3 effectively restored dexamethasone sensitivity. Earlier work by Hakonarson and colleagues using high-density oligonucleotide array analysis demonstrated that IL-1β and TNF-α induced a broad program of proinflammatory gene expression in ASM—including genes regulating transcription, cytokines, chemokines, and adhesion molecules—that dexamethasone largely repressed, while a small subset of genes was enhanced; these changes correlated with ASM contractility and relaxation responses, linking transcriptomic shifts to functional ASM phenotypes.

Long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs) and non-canonical mechanisms of glucocorticoid action also emerged as areas of interest. Austin and colleagues employed microarray-based mRNA and lncRNA profiling of ASMCs from healthy subjects and patients with nonsevere or severe asthma following dexamethasone and serum exposure, identifying plasmacytoma variant translocation 1 (PVT1) as a lncRNA whose expression was decreased in corticosteroid-sensitive nonsevere asthma but increased in corticosteroid-insensitive severe asthma; siRNA-mediated knockdown of PVT1 reduced both proliferation and IL-6 release in severe asthmatic ASMCs, implicating lncRNA dysregulation in steroid resistance and airway remodeling. Furthermore, Sasse and colleagues observed GR occupancy at loci including IL11 and LIF in the absence of supplemental ligand, with dexamethasone treatment abrogating antibody-GR complex detection at these sites in association with reduced RNA polymerase II occupancy, suggesting that non-canonical GR-chromatin interactions contribute to cytokine repression in ASM. An earlier study by Pierce and colleagues provided evidence that dexamethasone transcriptionally upregulated tropoelastin expression in fetal pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells and during late fetal lung development, linking glucocorticoid signaling to extracellular matrix remodeling in a developmental context.

## Common Methods

The predominant experimental approach across these studies was transcriptome-wide profiling, with methodological evolution evident across the time span covered. Earlier work employed high-density oligonucleotide microarrays to assess multi-gene expression changes in ASM cells treated with cytokines in the presence or absence of dexamethasone, while later studies adopted RNA sequencing (RNA-Seq) to achieve higher sensitivity and dynamic range in characterizing differentially expressed genes. Several studies supplemented transcriptomic analyses with quantitative RT-PCR to validate findings in additional cell lines, and with Western blotting or immunohistochemistry to confirm results at the protein level. In vitro cell culture models using primary human ASM cell lines were the predominant system, though murine models—including ovalbumin sensitization and challenge—were employed to interrogate the functional consequences of gene expression changes in vivo.

Beyond transcriptomics, chromatin-level approaches were applied to gain mechanistic insight into glucocorticoid receptor function. ChIP-Seq targeting GR and RNA polymerase II occupancy was used to establish direct transcriptional targets of GR in ASM and to identify active regulatory elements genome-wide. Gene ontology and pathway enrichment analyses were applied across studies to contextualize differentially expressed genes within biological processes relevant to ASM function, including cell proliferation, cytokine signaling, and hypertrophy. Functional perturbation experiments, such as siRNA-mediated gene knockdown and pharmacological inhibition of specific kinases (e.g., PLK1), were used in multiple studies to establish causal relationships between transcriptomic observations and cellular phenotypes including proliferation, contraction, and cytokine secretion.

## Gaps and Open Questions

Despite the breadth of transcriptomic data generated across these studies, the mechanistic basis of glucocorticoid resistance in severe or steroid-insensitive asthma remains incompletely understood. The observation by Austin and colleagues that PVT1 expression diverges between corticosteroid-sensitive and corticosteroid-insensitive patients, and the finding by Ouyang and colleagues that IL-17A synergizes with dexamethasone to paradoxically enhance CSF3 expression, highlight the complexity of steroid responsiveness at the transcriptomic level. However, it remains unclear which specific molecular features distinguish the glucocorticoid-regulated transcriptome in steroid-resistant versus steroid-sensitive ASM cells, and the relative contributions of epigenetic modifications, receptor isoform expression, and co-regulatory factors to these differences have not been fully resolved by the studies reviewed here.

Additionally, the functional consequences of the vast majority of differentially expressed genes identified in these transcriptomic datasets remain uncharacterized. Studies such as those by Himes and colleagues and Masuno and colleagues identified hundreds to thousands of dexamethasone-responsive genes in ASM, yet only a small subset—including KLF15, CRISPLD2, and PLCD1—have undergone detailed functional validation. The roles of other identified targets, such as those with anti-inflammatory annotations (IRS2, APPL2, RAMP1, MFGE8), remain largely unexplored in the ASM context. Furthermore, the non-canonical GR binding observed at IL11 and LIF loci in the absence of ligand, as reported by Sasse and colleagues, raises unresolved questions about the extent and biological significance of ligand-independent GR activity in ASM.

## Suggested Next Steps

Given the identification of multiple glucocorticoid-responsive genes without established functional roles in ASM, future research should prioritize systematic functional validation of transcriptomic candidates using loss- and gain-of-function approaches in both primary human ASM cells and relevant animal models. Particular attention should be directed toward genes identified across independent transcriptomic datasets, as convergence across studies—as seen with KLF15—may indicate biological significance. Integration of transcriptomic data with epigenomic profiling, such as ATAC-Seq or histone modification ChIP-Seq, could help to distinguish primary GR targets from secondary transcriptional cascades and to elucidate the chromatin landscape that determines context-specific glucocorticoid responses.

The mechanisms underlying steroid resistance in severe asthma represent a particularly pressing area for future investigation. Studies should examine whether the IL-17A/dexamethasone synergism observed at the CSF3 locus extends to other neutrophil-promoting genes, and whether therapeutic strategies combining low-dose glucocorticoids with cytokine pathway inhibitors can achieve durable efficacy without the adverse effects associated with high-dose steroid regimens. Similarly, the emerging role of lncRNAs such as PVT1 in modulating ASM phenotype warrants further investigation, including studies to define the molecular mechanisms by which PVT1 regulates proliferation and cytokine secretion,

## Papers Reviewed

1. Himes BE, Jiang X, Wagner P et al. (2014). RNA-Seq transcriptome profiling identifies CRISPLD2 as a glucocorticoid responsive gene that modulates cytokine function in airway smooth muscle cells.. *PloS one*. PMID: [24926665](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24926665/)

2. Pan Y, Xue Y, Fei X et al. (2025). PLK1 Mediates the Proliferation and Contraction of Airway Smooth Muscle Cells and Has a Role in T2-High Asthma with Neutrophilic Inflammation Model.. *Journal of inflammation research*. PMID: [40162075](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40162075/)

3. Sasse SK, Kadiyala V, Danhorn T et al. (2017). Glucocorticoid Receptor ChIP-Seq Identifies PLCD1 as a KLF15 Target that Represses Airway Smooth Muscle Hypertrophy.. *American journal of respiratory cell and molecular biology*. PMID: [28375666](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28375666/)

4. Hakonarson H, Halapi E, Whelan R et al. (2001). Association between IL-1beta/TNF-alpha-induced glucocorticoid-sensitive changes in multiple gene expression and altered responsiveness in airway smooth muscle.. *American journal of respiratory cell and molecular biology*. PMID: [11726403](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11726403/)

5. Ouyang S, Liu C, Xiao J et al. (2020). Targeting IL-17A/glucocorticoid synergy to CSF3 expression in neutrophilic airway diseases.. *JCI insight*. PMID: [32051346](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32051346/)

6. Masuno K, Haldar SM, Jeyaraj D et al. (2011). Expression profiling identifies Klf15 as a glucocorticoid target that regulates airway hyperresponsiveness.. *American journal of respiratory cell and molecular biology*. PMID: [21257922](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21257922/)

7. Pierce RA, Mariencheck WI, Sandefur S et al. (1995). Glucocorticoids upregulate tropoelastin expression during late stages of fetal lung development.. *The American journal of physiology*. PMID: [7900831](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7900831/)

8. Austin PJ, Tsitsiou E, Boardman C et al. (2017). Transcriptional profiling identifies the long noncoding RNA plasmacytoma variant translocation (PVT1) as a novel regulator of the asthmatic phenotype in human airway smooth muscle.. *The Journal of allergy and clinical immunology*. PMID: [27484035](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27484035/)
